exemplar.title: To what extent has inflation impacted the public transportation sector in Cameroon?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has inflation impacted the public transportation sector in Cameroon?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The topic and research question are communicated clearly and precisely, providing a focused foundation for the investigation.
The methodology section demonstrates informed selection of both primary (survey) and secondary (statistical and media) sources.
The research question remains effectively connected to each analytical subsection, ensuring consistency of focus.
weaknesses: Key methodological details (survey design, sampling frame, response rate, data-treatment methods) are missing or confined to the appendix, reducing transparency and reproducibility.
Several sections conclude abruptly (e.g., Aim section mid-sentence; analysis section ending at âinflationaryâ), disrupting narrative flow.
Some secondary data are quoted without sufficient context or unit clarification, limiting methodological completeness.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student clearly states their topic and research question and selects a reasonable mix of sources, significant gaps in methodological transparency and several abrupt, incomplete passages prevent full realization of the highest bands. These strengths and weaknesses align with the middle descriptors, justifying a score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Economic concepts (cost-push inflation, aggregate supply shifts, elasticity) are applied coherently and with generally appropriate relevance to the Cameroonian context.
Graphs, statistics, and media reports are used to illustrate claims, grounding theoretical discussion in concrete data.
Terminology and economic diagrams are employed accurately and consistently across sections.
weaknesses: Occasional reliance on unsourced assertions and blogâstyle material of uncertain reliability detracts from full credibility.
Some quantitative claims (e.g., magnitude of dead-weight loss) are stated without supporting calculations or citations.
Minor editing lapses (incomplete sentences) interrupt logical flow and undermine full coherence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates clear, coherent knowledge of economic concepts and applies them to source material in a generally relevant way. However, intermittent unsourced claims and lack of full triangulation keep the work from the highest band. A 4 out of 6 reflects solid, mostly effective use of knowledge with room for more rigorous support.

strengths: The majority of research sources (IMF data, INS notes, primary survey) are appropriate and directly relevant to the research question.
Primary survey findings are integrated to provide original empirical insight into service-quality impacts.
Supplyâdemand diagrams and figures effectively support analysis and help quantify user spending and ridership patterns.
weaknesses: Analytical depth is uneven: welfare-loss concepts are discussed qualitatively without quantification.
Evaluation of policy alternatives (electric vehicles, infrastructure initiatives) lacks feasibility analysis and cost/timeline assessment.
Some causal assertions (e.g., inflation leading to insecurity) are stated without empirical testing or citation of elasticity estimates.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work offers a coherent, logically structured analysis with relevant research integration, but key components of critical thinkingârobust quantification, deeper evaluation of alternatives, and empirical backing for causal claimsâare underdeveloped. This performance corresponds to the lower end of the 4â6 band, warranting 4 out of 12.

strengths: All required structural elements of an EE (title page, contents, introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion, references, appendices) are present and ordered logically.
Headings and subheadings guide the reader effectively through the investigationâs sections.
weaknesses: Caption numbering is irregular and long URLs break across lines, interrupting readability.
Tables and paragraphs split awkwardly across pages; the reference list retains HTML artifacts and lacks a uniform citation style.
Layout and formatting conventions are inconsistently applied, indicating missing or incorrect formatting considerations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the overall structure follows expectations for an extended essay, persistent formatting and layout issues prevent the work from reaching the top band. The combination of correct structure with flawed formatting aligns with the 1â2 band, justifying a score of 2 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question â âTo what extent has inflation impacted the public transportation sector in Cameroon?â â is explicitly stated, narrow in geographical scope and sectoral focus, and is referenced throughout each analytical section (fare adjustment, service quality, stakeholder effects).  The sub-headings map directly onto the elements implied by the question, so the discussion remains consistently anchored to it.  Because the question is neither too broad nor descriptive and clearly drives the investigation, it fulfils the highest descriptor for clarity and focus.

The student states the subject, broad topic and precise research question on the title page and reiterates them in the introduction, allowing the reader to understand instantly what is being investigated.  The background paragraph and subsequent Aim/Significance subsections explain why the issue is worth studying, delineate the 2019-24 timeframe and specify stakeholders, so the purpose and focus are fully appropriate for an EE-length systematic investigation.  Minor wording slips (e.g. heading labelled âWorthinessâ) do not impede the clear, accurate communication of topic or focus, so the work satisfies the top-band descriptor.

Most statistics, graphs and media reports are used to illustrate specific claimsâsuch as linking fuel-price data to fare adjustments or survey charts to consumer burdenâshowing generally relevant application.  Nonetheless, occasional reliance on unsourced assertions, blogs of uncertain reliability, and limited triangulation of primary data constrain full effectiveness.  These characteristics place the work solidly in the 3-4 band rather than the highest band that demands consistently appropriate application.

All required EE structural elements (title page, abstract omitted per new guide, contents, intro, methodology, analysis, conclusion, references, appendix) are present and logically ordered.  Headings and sub-headings guide the reader and analytical sections mirror the research question, demonstrating a clearly appropriate structure that meets top-band expectations.

The work shows awareness of EE layout conventionsâpage numbers, table of contents, labelled figures/tables, stated word countâbut execution is inconsistent.  Caption numbering is irregular, long URLs break lines, and tables/paragraphs split awkwardly across pages.  Reference list formatting retains HTML artefacts and lacks uniform style.  These shortcomings indicate that some required considerations are missing or incorrectly applied, corresponding to the 1-2 band.

Arguments progress logically from causes to impacts to policy responses, and the student does attempt evaluationâhighlighting trade-offs of fare adjustments and salary rises.  Yet critique is often brief, lacks alternative viewpoints, and does not systematically weigh quantitative evidence.  The conclusion restates findings but stops short of synthesising implications or limitations in depth.  This represents âreasoned argument with partially critical evaluationâ, consistent with a score near the middle of the 4-6 band.

The essay applies demandâsupply diagrams and elasticity reasoning to examine fare adjustments and stakeholder outcomes, providing coherent narrative links between theory and Cameroonian data.  However, depth is uneven: welfare-loss areas are described qualitatively without calculated estimates, and causal chains (e.g., from inflation to insecurity) are asserted rather than rigorously tested.  These limitations prevent the analysis from reaching the âeffective and well-supportedâ standard of the top band, situating it at 4 in the 4-6 range.

The student demonstrates a sound grasp of inflation, cost-push analysis, elasticity and welfare concepts, applying them coherently to the Cameroonian context.  Explanations of shifts in SRAS, price-floor effects and demand inelasticity are generally accurate.  Yet, some economic arguments remain descriptive (e.g., links between inflation and insecurity) and a few quantitative claims (dead-weight loss magnitude) are unsupported, indicating partially but not fully effective use of disciplinary knowledge.  This corresponds to the 3-4 band.

The majority of cited researchâIMF inflation series, INS price notes, government salary policy, primary surveyâdirectly addresses the research question and underpins subsequent analysis, indicating clear relevance.  Nevertheless, inclusion of promotional material (e.g., Yango earnings webpage) and blogs of uncertain scholarly value shows that selection is not consistently rigorous, aligning with the 4-6 band midpoint.

An appropriate mix of primary (Google-form survey) and secondary (IMF, INS, Statista, press articles) sources is identified, showing informed selection.  However, details of the questionnaire design, sampling frame, response rate and data treatment are sparse, and no transporter survey was executed, limiting methodological completeness.  Some secondary statistics (e.g., GDP from transport) are quoted without clarification of units or context.  These gaps align better with the middle band, where sources are âgenerally relevant â¦ with some evidence of informed selectionâ.

Economic terminologyââcost-push inflationâ, âaggregate supplyâ, âprice floorâ, âwelfare lossâ, âprice elasticityââis employed accurately and with consistency across sections.  Occasional lexical slips (e.g., âFurtherlyâ) do not undermine conceptual precision.  The sustained, correct use of graphs, variables (Pâ, Qâ) and sectoral jargon demonstrates strong command, meriting the top band, though isolated language errors justify awarding 5 rather than 6.

annotations.comment: The Table of Contents omits the page number for the main heading â1. Introduction,â reducing navigational clarity for the reader.
In-text citations are inconsistently formatted (e.g., parentheses style, missing page numbers), reducing the credibility and traceability of source material.
The Aim section ends mid-sentence, indicating incomplete content that leaves the research objectives and scope unclear.
Details on sampling frame, response rate, questionnaire design and data-treatment methods are absent, limiting methodological transparency and reproducibility.
The use of an <h1> tag for subsection 3.1 disrupts hierarchical consistency in heading levels; it should align with other h2 subheadings.
Details of the Minister of Commerceâs fare-adjustment announcement lack a formal citation, reducing verifiability of the policy reference.
The interpretation that COVID-19 had no impact on inflation is asserted without statistical or comparative analysis, limiting evaluative depth.
The theoretical discussion of fare adjustment is well-linked to the Cameroonian policy context, demonstrating sound application of economic concepts.
The cost-push inflation diagram clearly illustrates the SRAS shift and its effects on price level and real GDP, reinforcing the theoretical framework.
The methodology outlines a balanced mix of primary (questionnaire) and secondary (statistical and media) sources, showing informed selection of data.
The contents include all required sections and subsections, demonstrating a well-planned structure that mirrors the investigationâs scope.
The title and topic (âImpact of inflation in Cameroonâ) are communicated succinctly, immediately orienting the reader to the investigationâs focus.
The word count is provided and remains under the 4,000-word limit, satisfying the formatting requirement.
The background provides relevant context, citing IMF forecasts and national statistics to situate the study within current economic conditions.
The welfare-loss concept is described qualitatively without estimating the area or quantifying the economic magnitude, limiting analytical depth.
The research question is clearly and precisely stated on the title page, anchoring the investigation and demonstrating a focused approach to the topic.
Dot leaders and spacing in the contents listing are unevenly applied, resulting in inconsistent alignment across entries.
Secondary sources include informal blogs without discussion of their reliability or triangulation, weakening the overall research rigor.
Figure 1 effectively visualises the five-year inflation trend with clear data points and axis labels, aiding comprehension of key changes.
Quantitative details on gasoline and gas-oil price changes ground the analysis in concrete data and enhance credibility.
The term âFurtherlyâ appears in the discussion, indicating a lexical slip that should be revised for formal academic tone.
The conclusion candidly acknowledges language barriers and data limitations, demonstrating reflective engagement with research challenges.
Reflection remains largely descriptive; deeper evaluative insight into methodological setbacks and their impact on findings would strengthen engagement.
The footnote citation duplicates source details and lacks a consistent referencing style, reducing the professionalism of the presentation.
Assertions about inelastic demand lack empirical backing or citation of elasticity estimates, reducing the strength of the argument.
The analysis thoughtfully links government fare adjustments and salary hikes to potential effects on both transporters and users.
Mathematical notation uses HTML <math> tags instead of LaTeX syntax ($$â¦$$), which is inconsistent with academic presentation requirements.
The sentence explaining IMFs fuel subsidy pressure ends abruptly (ââ¦becauseâ), indicating incomplete editing and disrupting logical flow.
The analysis on transportersâ profitability generalises inelasticity effects without reconciling this with observed revenue data or broader market trends.
Feasibility, cost implications and timelines for proposed electric-vehicle and infrastructure initiatives are not assessed, limiting practical value.
The price-floor supplyâdemand diagram (Figure 2) effectively communicates the distributional and welfare effects of the fare adjustment.
Mathematical variables are rendered with <math> tags instead of LaTeX ($$P_{fl}$$), resulting in inconsistent notation across the report.
The discussion of service-quality implications demonstrates awareness of operational trade-offs under cost pressure and stakeholder impact.
The in-text citation format (âTeam, 2024â) and footnote placement are inconsistent, distracting from the narrative flow.
The section ends mid-sentence (âdid not result in aâ), indicating that content is missing and leaving the argument incomplete.
Integration of primary survey results to assess service quality adds original insight and strengthens the empirical basis of the evaluation.
Survey sample size, demographic breakdown and margin of error are not reported, limiting the reliability and generalisability of the findings.
Details of the primary survey instrument (questions, distribution method) are confined to the appendix, reducing methodological transparency in the main text.
The application of inelastic-demand theory to explain unchanged ridership and revenue outcomes for transporters demonstrates solid conceptual understanding.
Section 4.2 concludes mid-analysis, ending abruptly when introducing survey findings (ââ¦inflationaryâ), which disrupts the narrative flow.
Figures 3 and 4 are effectively deployed to quantify the impact of fare increases on user spending and ridership patterns.
Survey percentages are presented without context on the total respondent count or demographic composition, limiting interpretive validity.
Figure 5 clearly summarises alternative transport methods considered by users, highlighting behavioural changes under price pressure.
Reference entries on this page maintain inconsistent citation style, with missing authorship details and irregular title formatting.
References to crime statistics (assault, rape, accidents) lack formal sourcing or citation details, weakening the evidence base.
The 5% civil-servant salary increase is not compared to the prevailing inflation rate, preventing assessment of its real-wage impact.
Reliance on a 2018 source for private-sector workforce data is outdated; more recent employment statistics would better inform policy evaluation.
Recommendations to adopt electric vehicles and expand public transport options show creative, forward-looking engagement with policy solutions.
The conclusion largely restates earlier points without synthesising novel insights or policy implications, limiting its critical impact.
The reference list spans a diverse range of sourcesâinternational agencies, national statistics, academic studies and news outletsâreflecting extensive research.
Reference entries are inconsistently formatted, with mixed italics and missing publication details, reducing the listâs professional coherence.
Several URLs are broken across lines and lack hyperlink formatting, which hinders quick access to source documents.
